name: Improve Developer Productivity with GitHub Copilot
tagline: Generate, refactor, document, and test across the SDLC — and know the one rule that never changes: Copilot accelerates, humans verify.
overview: One of the 10–15% domains on GH-300. It tests whether you can put Copilot to work across the software lifecycle — writing everyday code, modernising legacy, generating docs and tests, getting debugging and security/performance help, and prototyping with Spark — and whether you keep the review discipline that makes that speed safe. The exam's favourite trap here is treating AI output as finished: generated tests you never read, a 'security fix' you never checked, an acceptance rate you mistake for value. The instinct it grades for is simple and recurs on every question: **Copilot gets you a first draft fast; a human still owns whether it's correct.**
objectives: Use completions and chat to generate everyday code, and refactor or modernise legacy code with Copilot's help.
Generate documentation and tests, and apply the review discipline that generated tests demand — read them, run them, judge coverage.
Get debugging, security, and performance assistance from Copilot, treating every suggestion as human-verified.
Reach for Spark to prototype an app or internal tool from a description, and know its sweet spot.
Measure Copilot's impact responsibly — never equate acceptance rate with value, and keep review in the loop.

lessons.title: Everyday generation, refactoring, and modernising legacy code
lessons.objective: Use Copilot to write and reshape code across the SDLC, verifying every change.
lessons.body:
## Where the everyday time goes

Most of a developer's day isn't novel algorithms — it's boilerplate, wiring, translating an intent into syntax, and improving code that already exists. Copilot targets exactly that layer.

- **Generation** — completions finish lines and blocks in flow; chat drafts a function, a class, or a whole module from a description. Fastest wins: scaffolding, repetitive structures, format conversions, glue code.
- **Refactoring** — select code and ask Copilot to make it more readable, split a long function, remove duplicated calculations, rewrite an awkward conditional, or improve names. The docs walk through each of these — and repeat one line after almost every example: *always check that the revised code runs without errors and produces the correct result.*
- **Modernising legacy code** — Copilot is strong at explaining unfamiliar or dated code (start with `/explain`) and then helping migrate it: to a newer language version, a different pattern, or a supported library. The move is **understand first, change second** — never let it rewrite code neither of you understands.

## The accelerate-then-verify loop

The pattern is the same every time: Copilot produces a **first draft** quickly; you **review, run, and correct** it. On legacy work especially, the review step is where the value is — the model can be confidently wrong about code it half-recognises.

## Why the exam cares

Productivity questions hand you a real task — 'convert this to the new API', 'tidy this function', 'scaffold this' — and ask how Copilot helps *and* what you must still do. The correct answer almost always includes the verification step. An option that skips it is the distractor.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You ask Copilot to refactor a long function into smaller pieces and it produces a clean version. What must you do before relying on it?

options.text: Run it and confirm it still produces the correct result — the same review any change needs
Nothing — a refactor by definition preserves behaviour
Merge it immediately so the diff stays small
Delete the tests, since the code is now cleaner

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Copilot gives you a first draft; **you verify it runs and behaves correctly**. A refactor is not guaranteed to preserve behaviour just because it looks tidy, merging blind skips review, and deleting tests removes the very safety net a refactor needs.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You're modernising a legacy module you don't fully understand. What's the safest first step with Copilot?

options.text: Use /explain to understand the existing code before changing anything
Let Agent mode rewrite it to the new pattern immediately, then read the diff
Accept completions until it compiles
Delete it and ask Copilot to write a replacement from the function name

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **Understand first, change second.** `/explain` builds the shared understanding you need before a migration. Rewriting code neither you nor the model understands, blindly accepting completions, or regenerating from a name alone all invite silent behaviour changes.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which task is squarely in Copilot's productivity sweet spot?

options.text: Scaffolding repetitive boilerplate and glue code from a clear description
Deciding the product's data-privacy strategy
Choosing which vendor to sign a contract with
Setting the team's quarterly priorities

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Copilot shines on well-specified, mechanical coding work like **boilerplate and glue**. Strategy, contracts, and prioritisation are human-judgment calls Copilot can inform but not own.

lessons.title: Generating documentation and tests — with the review that makes them safe
lessons.objective: Generate docs and tests fast, then apply the discipline generated tests demand.
lessons.body:
## Documentation, drafted in seconds

Copilot is quick to draft the docs developers skip when rushed: docstrings and comments (chat can add them across a selection), README sections, and explanations of what a module does. Treat the output as a **first draft to edit** — check it matches what the code actually does, not what the name implies.

## Tests: the biggest productivity lever, and the biggest trap

Ask Copilot for unit or integration tests and you get a suite in seconds — `/tests` in chat, ghost-text as you extend a test file, or Plan-then-Agent to plan and run a whole test workflow. The Microsoft Learn testing module frames it exactly this way: generate tests in chat, push for **edge cases**, use Plan and Agent modes to automate the run, and use ghost text to extend and **fix failing tests**.

Here's the discipline that separates real productivity from a false sense of it:

- **Read every generated test.** A test you didn't read is a claim you can't trust.
- **Judge what it actually covers.** Copilot may test the happy path and miss the edge cases that matter — the ones you'd have thought of yourself.
- **Run them, and make failing tests fail for the right reason.** A test that passes against buggy code, or asserts the current (wrong) behaviour, is worse than none.
- **Beware tests that just mirror the implementation.** A test written from the same misunderstanding as the code will pass and prove nothing.

## The rule, stated plainly

Generated tests **accelerate** test-writing; they don't **replace** the judgement about what to test and whether the assertions are right. That judgement stays with you.

## Why the exam cares

This is the domain's signature scenario: 'Copilot generated 40 passing tests — ship it?' The graded answer is never 'yes, they pass'. It's review the tests, check coverage and edge cases, and confirm they'd fail on a real bug.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Copilot generates a unit-test suite and every test passes. What's the responsible next step?

options.text: Read the tests, check they cover edge cases, and confirm they'd fail against buggy code
Merge immediately — passing tests prove the code is correct
Increase the number of tests until the count looks impressive
Delete the assertions so the suite runs faster

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Passing generated tests can be testing the wrong thing — or mirroring a bug. **Review them, judge coverage and edge cases, and verify they catch real failures.** Volume isn't value, and stripping assertions guts the test.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Why is a Copilot-generated test that simply mirrors the implementation a problem?

options.text: It can pass even when the code is wrong, because it encodes the same misunderstanding — proving nothing
It runs too slowly to be useful
Copilot cannot generate tests that reference the implementation
It always fails on the first run

knowledgeCheck.explanation: A test derived from the same assumptions as the code will **pass in lockstep with the bug** — it validates the mistake instead of catching it. That's why you judge whether tests assert intended behaviour, not just current behaviour.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You want Copilot to draft docstrings across a file. How should you treat the result?

options.text: As a first draft to review and correct against what the code actually does
As authoritative documentation that needs no checking
As a reason to remove the code comments you already had
As a substitute for understanding the code yourself

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Generated docs are a **fast first draft you edit** — verify they describe real behaviour, not the behaviour the name suggests. They're not automatically authoritative and don't replace your own understanding.

lessons.title: Debugging, security, and performance help — always human-verified
lessons.objective: Use Copilot to debug and to spot security/performance issues, verifying every suggestion.
lessons.body:
## Debugging with Copilot

When something breaks, Copilot shortens the loop:

- **Explain the error.** Paste a stack trace or error message and ask what it means and likely causes — faster than a search, and grounded in your code with `#`-references.
- **`/fix`.** Ask chat to propose a fix for the selected code or the reported problem. You get a candidate diff to review — not a guaranteed cure.
- **Reason about behaviour.** Ask Copilot to trace how a value flows, or why a branch is taken, to narrow a bug down.

The candidate fix is a hypothesis. You still confirm it addresses the *root cause* rather than masking a symptom, and that it doesn't break something else — which is what your tests are for.

## Security and performance: spot, suggest, verify

Copilot can flag a likely SQL-injection risk, a missing input check, or an obviously inefficient loop, and suggest an improvement. Used well, it's an extra pair of eyes that catches easy misses early.

But two hard limits:

- **It is not a security scanner or a profiler.** A clean chat reply is not proof there are no vulnerabilities or bottlenecks — absence of a warning is not a guarantee. Real assurance comes from dedicated tooling (SAST, load tests, profilers) and human review.
- **Every suggested 'fix' is human-verified.** A performance rewrite must be measured, not assumed faster; a security fix must be checked, not trusted because Copilot proposed it.

## The through-line

Debugging, security, and performance are all the same shape: **Copilot accelerates diagnosis and drafts a fix; you verify it's correct and complete.** The speed is real; the accountability doesn't move.

## Why the exam cares

Expect a suggestion framed as done — 'Copilot said it's now secure', 'Copilot's version is faster'. The right answer treats it as a candidate to verify (measure it, scan it, test it), never as a finished guarantee.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Copilot proposes a fix via /fix and it makes the failing test pass. What still matters?

options.text: Confirming the fix addresses the root cause and doesn't break anything else, not just that one test goes green
Nothing — a green test means the bug is fully resolved
Reverting your own tests so Copilot's fix owns the file
Disabling other tests that might now fail

knowledgeCheck.explanation: A `/fix` is a **hypothesis**. One passing test can mask a symptom rather than cure the cause — verify the root cause and run the full suite. Disabling tests to hide regressions is the opposite of verification.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Copilot reviewed a function in chat and didn't mention any security issues. What can you conclude?

options.text: Little — Copilot isn't a security scanner; absence of a warning isn't proof the code is safe
The code is guaranteed free of vulnerabilities
You can skip your SAST scan for this change
The function is safe to expose publicly without further review

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Copilot can **spot** likely issues but gives no completeness guarantee — **no warning is not a clean bill of health.** Real assurance comes from dedicated security tooling and human review, which you don't skip on Copilot's silence.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Copilot rewrites a hot loop and says it's more efficient. How do you treat that claim?

options.text: As a candidate to measure — benchmark before and after to confirm it's actually faster
As fact, because Copilot analysed the code
As irrelevant, since performance never matters
As a reason to remove the old version without testing correctness

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Performance claims are **verified by measurement**, not assumption — profile or benchmark to confirm the gain (and that behaviour is unchanged). Trusting the claim blind, or dropping the tested version, skips the verify step.

lessons.title: Prototyping with Spark, and measuring impact responsibly
lessons.objective: Use Spark for quick builds and measure Copilot's value honestly.
lessons.body:
## Spark: an idea to a running app, fast

**GitHub Spark** turns a natural-language description into a full-stack web app — data storage, AI features, and GitHub authentication built in — with one-click deployment. Its pitch is speed: **validate ideas in hours instead of months.** That makes it a natural fit for **prototypes, quick internal tools, and demos** — get something running, then iterate with prompts, visual tools, or code. It doesn't turn a prototype into a hardened production system for free; the same review and engineering rigour still apply before anything real depends on it.

## Measuring impact without fooling yourself

Copilot clearly saves time — but *how much*, and *at what quality*, needs honest measurement. The classic mistake is treating **acceptance rate** (how often suggestions are accepted) as the headline value metric. It isn't:

- An accepted suggestion can still be **wrong**, and get corrected — or worse, not corrected — later.
- A **rejected** suggestion can still have saved time by sparking the right idea.
- High acceptance with weak review can *increase* downstream defects — motion without progress.

Better signals combine perception and outcomes: developer-reported time saved and satisfaction, cycle time and throughput, and crucially **quality held constant** — because speed that ships more bugs isn't productivity. And none of it removes the constant: **review stays in the loop.** The gain is real only when a human still verifies what lands.

## Where Copilot helps most — and where humans dominate

Copilot's leverage is highest on well-specified, mechanical, verifiable work: boilerplate, tests, docs, routine refactors, first-draft fixes. Human judgement dominates where the problem is ambiguous, high-stakes, or architectural — deciding *what* to build, security-critical design, novel algorithms, live incidents. Knowing which side of that line a task sits on is itself the productivity skill.

## Why the exam cares

Expect a metrics trap — 'acceptance rate is 80%, so Copilot is working' — and a Spark placement question. The graded instincts: **don't equate acceptance with value, keep review in the loop, and match Spark to prototypes/internal tools.**

knowledgeCheck.stem: What is GitHub Spark best suited for?

options.text: Quickly building a prototype or internal tool from a natural-language description
Reviewing pull requests for security issues
Storing an organisation's Copilot policies
Excluding files from Copilot

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **Spark** builds and deploys full-stack apps from prose — its sweet spot is **prototypes and internal tools**, validating ideas fast. The others describe code review, admin policy, and content exclusion.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A manager reports 'our Copilot acceptance rate is 80%, so it's clearly boosting productivity'. What's the flaw?

options.text: Acceptance rate isn't value — accepted code can still be wrong, and high acceptance with weak review can add defects
80% is too low to mean anything
Acceptance rate can only be measured on the Enterprise plan
Nothing — acceptance rate is the definitive productivity metric

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **Acceptance rate measures usage, not value.** Accepted suggestions can be defective, rejected ones can still help, and acceptance without review can raise defect rates. Real measurement pairs time-saved and satisfaction with quality held constant.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which task leans most on human judgement rather than Copilot's automation?

options.text: Deciding the security-critical architecture of a new payment flow
Generating boilerplate DTOs from a schema
Drafting docstrings for existing functions
Writing a first-pass unit test for a pure function

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Ambiguous, high-stakes, architectural work — like a **security-critical payment design** — is where human judgement dominates. Boilerplate, docs, and first-pass tests are exactly where Copilot's leverage is highest (still reviewed).

questions.stem: What is Copilot's core value proposition for everyday productivity, stated correctly?

options.text: It produces a fast first draft; a human still verifies whether it's correct
It produces finished, correct code that needs no review
It replaces the need for tests
It guarantees the code is secure and optimal

questions.explanation: The recurring theme: **Copilot accelerates, humans verify.** It drafts fast; it doesn't remove review, tests, or accountability for correctness and security.

questions.stem: Which everyday task is the strongest fit for Copilot code generation?

options.text: Scaffolding repetitive boilerplate from a clear description
Deciding which features to prioritise this quarter
Negotiating a vendor contract
Choosing the company's cloud provider

questions.explanation: Mechanical, well-specified coding like **boilerplate** is Copilot's sweet spot. Prioritisation, contracts, and vendor choices are human-judgment decisions.

questions.stem: Before relying on a Copilot refactor, the documentation says you should always do what?

options.text: Check that the revised code runs without errors and produces the correct result
Assume behaviour is preserved because it's a refactor
Merge it before anyone can object
Remove the existing tests

questions.explanation: The refactoring docs repeat it after nearly every example: **verify the revised code runs and is correct.** A tidy-looking refactor is not guaranteed to preserve behaviour.

questions.stem: You're migrating a legacy module to a newer API. What's the recommended first move with Copilot?

options.text: Use /explain to understand the existing code before changing it
Let it rewrite everything first, then try to understand the diff
Accept completions until it compiles
Regenerate the module from its file name

questions.explanation: **Understand first, change second.** `/explain` gives you the grasp needed to judge a migration; rewriting or regenerating code you don't understand invites silent behaviour changes.

questions.stem: Which Copilot Chat command asks for tests for the selected code?

options.text: /tests
/deploy
/secure
/benchmark

questions.explanation: `/tests` requests tests for the current selection or context. The others aren't Copilot Chat test commands.

questions.stem: Copilot generates 40 unit tests and they all pass. Which conclusion is sound?

options.text: You still need to read them and judge coverage — passing doesn't prove they test the right things
The feature is fully verified because all tests pass
More passing tests always means better quality
You can delete manual review from the process

questions.explanation: Generated tests can pass while testing the wrong things or mirroring a bug. **Read them and judge coverage and edge cases** — count and green status aren't proof of quality.

questions.stem: Why push Copilot to generate edge-case tests specifically?

options.text: Generated tests often cover the happy path but miss the boundary conditions where real bugs hide
Edge-case tests run faster than normal tests
Copilot refuses to write happy-path tests
Boundary tests never fail, so they're safe to add

questions.explanation: Copilot's default output leans to the **happy path**; the boundary and error conditions are where defects lurk, so you explicitly ask for them — as the testing module recommends.

questions.stem: A generated test passes against the current code but the code has a known bug. What does that most likely indicate?

options.text: The test asserts the current (buggy) behaviour instead of the intended behaviour
The test framework is misconfigured
Copilot cannot generate assertions
The bug has been fixed automatically

questions.explanation: A test that **encodes the current behaviour** will pass in lockstep with the bug — it validates the mistake. That's why you check tests assert *intended* behaviour and would fail on the defect.

questions.stem: How should you treat Copilot-generated docstrings and README sections?

options.text: As a first draft to review against what the code actually does
As authoritative documentation needing no checks
As a replacement for understanding the code
As a reason to delete existing accurate comments

questions.explanation: Generated docs are a **fast draft you edit** — verify they describe real behaviour, not the name's implication. They aren't automatically authoritative and don't replace understanding.

questions.stem: You paste a stack trace into Copilot Chat. What is the realistic benefit?

options.text: A faster explanation of the error and likely causes to investigate
A guaranteed correct fix that needs no verification
Automatic deployment of a patch
Proof the bug cannot recur

questions.explanation: Explaining an error and suggesting causes **speeds diagnosis** — but it's a starting point to investigate, not a guaranteed cure, an auto-deploy, or a permanent fix.

questions.stem: Copilot's /fix makes a failing test go green. What still needs checking?

options.text: That it fixes the root cause and doesn't break other behaviour — run the full suite
Nothing further; one green test closes the bug
That the fix is the longest possible solution
That other failing tests are disabled

questions.explanation: A `/fix` is a **candidate**. One passing test can mask a symptom — confirm the root cause and run everything. Disabling tests to hide regressions is not verification.

questions.stem: Copilot didn't flag any vulnerabilities when you asked it to review a function. What follows?

options.text: Nothing conclusive — Copilot isn't a security scanner, so no warning isn't a guarantee of safety
The code is provably secure
You can skip dedicated security tooling for it
It's safe to expose publicly with no further review

questions.explanation: Copilot can **spot** likely issues but offers no completeness guarantee. **Absence of a warning is not a clean bill of health** — dedicated scanners and human review still apply.

questions.stem: Copilot proposes a rewrite it describes as faster. What's the responsible way to accept it?

options.text: Benchmark before and after to confirm the gain and that behaviour is unchanged
Accept it as fact because Copilot analysed the code
Ignore performance entirely
Delete the old version without testing correctness

questions.explanation: Performance is **verified by measurement**, not assertion — benchmark to confirm the improvement and preserved behaviour before dropping the old code.

questions.stem: What does GitHub Spark do?

options.text: Builds and deploys a full-stack app from a natural-language description
Runs Copilot code review on pull requests
Manages content-exclusion rules
Stores org-wide Copilot policies

questions.explanation: **Spark** turns prose into a deployable full-stack app (storage, AI, auth built in). The other options are code review, exclusions, and admin policy.

questions.stem: You need a quick internal tool to validate an idea this week. Which fits best?

options.text: GitHub Spark — describe it and get a running app to iterate on
A Copilot Space of screenshots
Content exclusions on the repo
A PR summary

questions.explanation: **Spark** is built for exactly this — validate ideas fast with a working prototype or internal tool. Spaces, exclusions, and PR summaries don't build an app.

questions.stem: Which is the best way to measure Copilot's productivity impact?

options.text: Combine developer-reported time saved and satisfaction with outcome metrics, holding quality constant
Use suggestion acceptance rate as the single headline metric
Count total lines of code Copilot wrote
Measure how many suggestions were shown per hour

questions.explanation: Honest measurement blends **perception and outcomes with quality held constant.** Acceptance rate, lines written, and suggestions shown all measure activity, not value.

questions.stem: Why is suggestion acceptance rate a weak measure of value on its own?

options.text: Accepted suggestions can be wrong, rejected ones can still help, and high acceptance with weak review can add defects
It can't be collected accurately
It's only available to enterprise admins
It always understates the real benefit

questions.explanation: Acceptance rate tracks **usage, not correctness or outcomes** — accepted code may be defective, rejected code may still spark the right answer, and unreviewed acceptance can raise defects.

questions.stem: Which task sits on the human-judgement side of the line, where Copilot assists but doesn't own the call?

options.text: Designing the security architecture of a new authentication system
Generating getters and setters for a data class
Writing a docstring for an existing function
Drafting a first unit test for a pure function

questions.explanation: Ambiguous, high-stakes, architectural work — **security-critical design** — is human-led. Boilerplate, docs, and first-pass tests are where Copilot's leverage is highest (still reviewed).

questions.stem: Which practices keep Copilot-generated tests trustworthy? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Read every generated test rather than trusting a green run
Check coverage and push for edge cases, not just the happy path
Confirm the tests would actually fail against buggy code
Accept any suite that passes, since passing means correct

questions.explanation: Trustworthy generated tests are **read, coverage-checked, and proven to catch real failures.** 'It passes, so it's correct' is exactly the trap — passing tests can mirror a bug or miss edge cases.

questions.stem: Across generation, refactoring, tests, debugging, and security help, what's the constant that every correct answer shares? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Copilot produces a first draft or candidate quickly
A human reviews and verifies before it's relied on
Accountability for correctness stays with the developer
AI output can be shipped unreviewed once it looks right

questions.explanation: The domain's through-line: Copilot **drafts fast**, a **human verifies**, and **accountability doesn't move.** 'Looks right, ship it unreviewed' is the anti-pattern the exam tests against.

scenarios.title: A safe test-generation workflow
scenarios.tagline: Turn 'Copilot wrote the tests' into tests you can actually trust.
scenarios.context: You've just finished a payments helper module and want a solid unit-test suite fast. You decide to generate tests with Copilot — but do it responsibly. Work each step.

steps.prompt: You're ready to create the initial suite. How do you start?

choices.text: Use /tests in chat to generate a first suite from the module
choices.rationale: Generating a first draft with /tests is exactly the fast start Copilot is for — you'll review it next.

choices.text: Write every test by hand and never involve Copilot
choices.rationale: Perfectly correct, just slower — you're leaving the drafting speed on the table.

choices.text: Skip tests entirely because the code looks fine
choices.rationale: No tests on a payments module is the opposite of safe; 'looks fine' isn't verification.

steps.prompt: Copilot returns a suite and every test passes on the first run. What now?

choices.text: Read each test and check what it actually asserts before trusting the green run
choices.rationale: A test you didn't read is a claim you can't trust — reviewing them is the core discipline.

choices.text: Merge immediately — all green means the module is verified
choices.rationale: Passing generated tests can assert the wrong behaviour or mirror a bug; green isn't proof.

choices.text: Glance at the test names and assume the bodies are fine
choices.rationale: Names hint at intent but the assertions are where correctness lives — a skim isn't enough.

steps.prompt: Reading them, you notice they only cover typical amounts — no zero, no negative, no overflow. What do you do?

choices.text: Ask Copilot to add edge-case tests for zero, negative, and boundary amounts, then review those too
choices.rationale: Edge cases are where payment bugs hide; prompting for them and reviewing is the right move.

choices.text: Leave it — the happy path passing is good enough
choices.rationale: Happy-path-only coverage misses exactly the boundaries that break payments code.

choices.text: Add the edge cases entirely by hand instead
choices.rationale: Correct and thorough, just slower than letting Copilot draft them for you to review.

steps.prompt: You want confidence the suite would actually catch a regression. How do you check?

choices.text: Introduce a deliberate bug and confirm a test fails — then revert
choices.rationale: Proving the tests fail on a real defect is the strongest confirmation they're meaningful.

choices.text: Trust that passing tests must also fail correctly on bugs
choices.rationale: Passing says nothing about whether a test can detect a defect — you have to check.

choices.text: Re-run the passing suite a second time to be sure
choices.rationale: Confirms stability, but re-running green tests doesn't prove they'd catch a regression.

scenarios.title: Modernise a legacy service — accelerate, then verify
scenarios.tagline: Speed up a migration without letting Copilot change what you don't understand.
scenarios.context: You've inherited an old order-processing service that must move to a supported library and shed some dead weight. You'll lean on Copilot to go fast — while keeping verification in the loop at every step.

steps.prompt: You barely know this code. What's the first thing you do with Copilot?

choices.text: Use /explain on the key modules to understand current behaviour
choices.rationale: Understand first, change second — /explain builds the grasp a migration needs.

choices.text: Have Agent mode migrate the whole service now, and read the diff after
choices.rationale: Rewriting code you don't understand risks silent behaviour changes you can't catch in review.

choices.text: Start renaming things until it feels familiar
choices.rationale: Better names help, but that's not understanding the behaviour you're about to migrate.

steps.prompt: Before you change anything, what makes the migration safest to verify later?

choices.text: Generate a characterisation test suite for current behaviour and review it, so you can detect regressions
choices.rationale: Tests that pin down today's behaviour give you a safety net to verify the migration against.

choices.text: Nothing — you'll eyeball the diff at the end
choices.rationale: Eyeballing a legacy migration by diff alone won't catch subtle behaviour changes.

choices.text: Write a couple of smoke tests by hand and call it covered
choices.rationale: Some coverage beats none, but thin coverage will miss the edge cases legacy code hides.

steps.prompt: Copilot proposes the migration to the new library. How do you accept it?

choices.text: Review the diff, run the characterisation tests, and confirm behaviour is unchanged
choices.rationale: The candidate change is verified against your tests before you trust it — accelerate then verify.

choices.text: Accept it wholesale because it compiles
choices.rationale: Compiling isn't correct — a migration can compile and still change behaviour.

choices.text: Accept it but only spot-check one function
choices.rationale: A spot-check is partial assurance; running the suite is what actually confirms it.

labs.title: Generate a test suite you can actually trust
labs.goal: Practise the accelerate-then-verify loop on generated tests.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

Pick a small, pure function in a repo you know and build its tests with Copilot — responsibly.

1. **Generate** — use `/tests` in chat to create a first suite. Note how fast the draft appears.
2. **Read** — go through every test and check what it actually asserts, not just that it's green.
3. **Cover the edges** — ask Copilot for boundary and error-case tests (empty, zero, negative, overflow) and review those too.
4. **Prove it bites** — introduce a deliberate bug, run the suite, and confirm a test fails. Revert the bug.

## Why it matters

The exam's signature D5 trap is 'the tests pass, so ship it'. Doing this once makes the discipline muscle-memory: generated tests are a fast draft you read, extend, and prove.
checklist: Every test read and its assertions understood
A deliberate bug made a test fail, then reverted

labs.title: Refactor and document with the verification step built in
labs.goal: Feel how Copilot accelerates cleanup while you own correctness.
labs.steps:
## Steps

Take one long, messy function you understand.

1. **Explain** — run `/explain` to confirm your understanding of what it does today.
2. **Refactor** — ask Copilot to split it into smaller functions and improve the names. Review the diff.
3. **Verify** — run the code (and any tests) to confirm the result is unchanged — the step the docs demand after every refactor.
4. **Document** — have Copilot draft docstrings for the new functions, then edit them to match real behaviour.

## Checkpoint

If the refactor changed behaviour and your run caught it, you've just seen why 'looks tidy' is never 'verified'. That catch is the whole point.
checklist: Reviewed the refactor diff before accepting
Ran it to confirm behaviour is unchanged
Edited generated docstrings to match real behaviour

resources.title: GitHub Copilot tutorials (cookbook) — tasks across the SDLC
resources.annotation: Task-based guides for generation, refactoring, docs, tests, debugging, and security — the practical spine of this domain. Every recipe ends with reviewing the result.

resources.title: Develop unit tests using GitHub Copilot tools (MS Learn)
resources.annotation: Hands-on: generate tests in chat, target edge cases, use Plan and Agent modes to run test workflows, and extend/fix failing tests with ghost text. The test-generation module to work end to end.

resources.title: Refactor code with GitHub Copilot
resources.annotation: Worked refactors — split functions, remove duplication, rewrite conditionals, improve names — each closing with the same rule: always check the revised code runs and is correct.

resources.title: GitHub Spark
resources.annotation: Build and deploy a full-stack app from natural language — storage, AI, and auth built in. Its pitch is speed: validate ideas in hours, ideal for prototypes and internal tools.

resources.title: Copilot code review & PR summaries
resources.annotation: AI review always leaves a 'Comment' (never Approve/Request changes), doesn't count toward required approvals, and won't block a merge — assistive, with a human accountable.

resources.title: Review AI-generated code (GitHub Copilot tutorial)
resources.annotation: The validate-the-output discipline in practice: pair automated tests and static analysis with human judgement — functional, context, quality, and dependency checks — before trusting generated code.

videos.title: D5 · Generate, refactor & document — accelerate then verify
D5 · Test generation & measuring impact responsibly
